data.incorrect: I am speaking to you like your father.
data.correct: I am speaking to you as your father.

data.incorrect: She sings as an angel.
data.correct: She sings like an angel.

title: Unit 55: Used to / Be used to

content.explanation:
### Diferentes usos de "Used to"

Cuidado, "used to" puede significar tres cosas completamente distintas dependiendo de lo que vaya antes o después.

#### 1. Used to + Verbo Base (Solía)
Un hábito en el pasado que ya no es cierto.
*   *Example:* I **used to play** tennis. (Yo solía jugar tenis, ya no lo hago).
*   *Negativo:* I **didn't use to** smoke.

#### 2. Be used to + V-ing / Sustantivo (Estar acostumbrado a)
Un estado en el que algo ya no es nuevo o extraño para ti.
*   *Example:* I **am used to waking** up early. (Estoy acostumbrado a despertar temprano. No me molesta).
*   *Example:* She **isn't used to** the cold weather. (No está acostumbrada al clima frío).

#### 3. Get used to + V-ing / Sustantivo (Acostumbrarse a)
El *proceso* de adaptación. Algo se está volviendo normal.
*   *Example:* I recently moved to London, I am **getting used to** driving on the left. (Me estoy acostumbrando).

---
Domina estas tres estructuras vitales en los ejercicios.

data.incorrect: I didn't used to like vegetables.
data.correct: I didn't use to like vegetables.

exercises.instruction: Be used to requiere -ing o sustantivo.

data.incorrect: He is used to drive fast.
data.correct: He is used to driving fast.

exercises.instruction: Hábito pasado no usa verbo To Be.

data.incorrect: I was used to play basketball when I was young.
data.correct: I used to play basketball when I was young.

data.incorrect: You will used to the noise.
data.correct: You will get used to the noise.

data.incorrect: I didn't used to the dark.
data.correct: I wasn't used to the dark.

content.explanation:
### Enfatizadores: So vs Such

Usamos *So* y *Such* para dar mucho énfasis a lo que decimos, significan "tan" o "tal".

#### SO + Adjetivo / Adverbio
Usamos **So** cuando después viene solo un adjetivo o un adverbio. NO hay un sustantivo.
*   *Example:* She is **so beautiful**. (Es tan hermosa).
*   *Example:* He runs **so fast**.

#### SUCH + (Adjetivo) + Sustantivo
Usamos **Such** cuando el énfasis recae sobre un **sustantivo** (una cosa o persona), incluso si hay un adjetivo en medio.
*   *Example:* She is **such a beautiful girl**. (Como termina en "girl", que es sustantivo, usamos Such).
*   *Example:* They are **such good friends**.

#### El resultado (That...)
A menudo los usamos para mostrar la consecuencia de algo.
*   It was **so** cold **that** the water froze.
*   It was **such** a cold day **that** the water froze.

---
Concéntrate en la última palabra de la frase en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: It was so a good movie.
data.correct: It was such a good movie.

data.incorrect: They are such a nice people.
data.correct: They are such nice people.

data.incorrect: It is such long book.
data.correct: It is such a long book.

data.incorrect: I was so tired what I slept immediately.
data.correct: I was so tired that I slept immediately.

data.incorrect: Those are such a big shoes.
data.correct: Those are such big shoes.

content.explanation:
### Opciones y Negaciones (Either & Neither)

Estas palabras se usan para hablar de **dos opciones**.

#### Either ... Or (O lo uno ... O lo otro)
Ofrece una opción entre dos cosas.
*   *Example:* You can have **either** tea **or** coffee. (Puedes tomar o té o café, elige uno).

#### Neither ... Nor (Ni lo uno ... Ni lo otro)
Negación doble. Rechaza ambas opciones. Cuidado: el verbo debe ir en afirmativo porque "Neither" ya es negativo.
*   *Example:* He likes **neither** tea **nor** coffee. (No le gusta ni el té ni el café).
*   *Incorrecto:* He *doesn't* like neither...

#### Both ... And (Ambos ... Y)
Acepta las dos opciones a la vez.
*   *Example:* I speak **both** English **and** French.

---
Elige sabiamente en estos 10 ejercicios.

data.incorrect: Neither John or Peter came.
data.correct: Neither John nor Peter came.

data.incorrect: I don't speak neither Spanish nor Italian.
data.correct: I speak neither Spanish nor Italian.

data.incorrect: We can either eat pizza nor pasta.
data.correct: We can either eat pizza or pasta.

data.incorrect: Both my parents is doctors.
data.correct: Both my parents are doctors.

data.incorrect: He is both smart or funny.
data.correct: He is both smart and funny.

content.explanation:
### Cantidades y Totalidades

Saber qué palabra usar depende de **cuántas cosas** estés hablando.

#### Hablando de DOS cosas:
*   **Both:** Ambos (100% de 2). *Both cars are fast.*
*   **Neither:** Ninguno de los dos (0% de 2). *Neither car is cheap.*
*   **Either:** Cualquiera de los dos. *Either car is fine.*

#### Hablando de TRES o MÁS cosas:
*   **All:** Todos (100% de 3+). *All the students passed.*
*   **None:** Ninguno (0% de 3+). *None of the students failed.*
*   **Every:** Cada (Uno por uno, siempre lleva verbo en singular). *Every student is happy.*

---
¡Cuenta bien antes de elegir tu respuesta!

exercises.instruction: Todos los de un grupo grande.

data.incorrect: Every students are here.
data.correct: Every student is here.

exercises.instruction: Para 2 cosas se usa Neither, no None.

data.incorrect: I have two brothers, none of them is married.
data.correct: I have two brothers, neither of them is married.

exercises.instruction: Para 3+ cosas se usa All, no Both.

data.incorrect: I have three cats, both of them are black.
data.correct: I have three cats, all of them are black.

exercises.instruction: Ninguno de los dos usa verbo singular (formalmente).

data.incorrect: Neither of my two cars work.
data.correct: Neither of my two cars works.

data.incorrect: Every of us went to the beach.
data.correct: All of us went to the beach.

content.explanation:
### Posesión: Have vs Have got

Para decir que posees algo, puedes usar "Have" o "Have got". El significado es exactamente el mismo, pero cambian gramaticalmente al hacer preguntas o negar. "Have got" es muy común en el inglés británico.

#### HAVE (El verbo normal)
Para preguntar o negar, necesita el auxiliar **do/does**.
*   *Afirmativo:* I **have** a car.
*   *Negativo:* I **don't have** a car.
*   *Pregunta:* **Do** you **have** a car?

#### HAVE GOT (Con auxiliar integrado)
Aquí "Have" actúa como auxiliar, así que no necesitas "do".
*   *Afirmativo:* I **have got** a car. (O I've got a car).
*   *Negativo:* I **haven't got** a car.
*   *Pregunta:* **Have** you **got** a car?

*(Cuidado: En pasado, siempre usamos "had" o "didn't have". Nunca "had got" para posesión).*

---
Evita mezclar las estructuras en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: I haven't a car.
data.correct: I don't have a car.

data.incorrect: Have you a pen?
data.correct: Do you have a pen?

data.incorrect: Do you have got a problem?
data.correct: Have you got a problem?

data.incorrect: I had got a dog when I was young.
data.correct: I had a dog when I was young.

data.incorrect: He doesn't has a job.
data.correct: He doesn't have a job.

content.explanation:
### Prefijos y Sufijos

Dominar las partes de las palabras multiplicará tu vocabulario drásticamente.

#### Prefijos (Cambian el significado, a menudo a negativo)
*   **Un- / Dis- / Im-:** Significan NO.
    *   *Happy -> Unhappy*
    *   *Agree -> Disagree*
    *   *Possible -> Impossible*
*   **Re-:** Hacer de nuevo.
    *   *Write -> Rewrite*

#### Sufijos (Cambian el tipo de palabra: adjetivo, sustantivo)
*   **-less:** Sin (Convierte a adjetivo negativo).
    *   *Use -> Useless* (Inútil).
*   **-ful:** Lleno de (Convierte a adjetivo positivo).
    *   *Hope -> Hopeful* (Esperanzado).
*   **-ness:** Convierte un adjetivo en un sustantivo abstracto.
    *   *Happy -> Happiness* (Felicidad).

---
Construye las palabras correctas en estos ejercicios finales del bloque.

exercises.instruction: Lo opuesto a Agree es Disagree.

data.incorrect: I unagree with you.
data.correct: I disagree with you.

data.incorrect: That is unpossible.
data.correct: That is impossible.

data.incorrect: Please be care.
data.correct: Please be careful.

data.incorrect: He is a unhome person.
data.correct: He is a homeless person.

data.incorrect: Don't be so unpatient.
data.correct: Don't be so impatient.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con GET

El verbo "Get" es el más versátil (y confuso) del inglés. Combinado con preposiciones, crea docenas de significados nuevos.

Aquí están los 5 más importantes para dominar:
1.  **Get over:** Superar (una enfermedad, una ruptura amorosa o un miedo).
    *   *I finally got over my cold.* (Al fin superé mi resfriado).
2.  **Get away with:** Salirse con la suya (hacer algo malo sin recibir castigo).
    *   *He cheated on the test and got away with it.*
3.  **Get along (with):** Llevarse bien (con alguien).
    *   *I don't get along with my boss.*
4.  **Get by:** Sobrevivir o arreglárselas (financieramente o en una situación difícil).
    *   *My Spanish is bad, but I get by.* (Me las arreglo).
5.  **Get across:** Hacer que algo se entienda (comunicar un mensaje).
    *   *It's hard to get my point across in French.*

---
Memorízalos usando estos ejercicios de contexto.

data.incorrect: I don't get away with my sister.
data.correct: I don't get along with my sister.

data.incorrect: I don't have much money, but I get over.
data.correct: I don't have much money, but I get by.

data.incorrect: She still hasn't got along with her ex-boyfriend.
data.correct: She still hasn't got over her ex-boyfriend.

exercises.instruction: Salirse con la suya en el pasado.

data.incorrect: He lied and got along with it.
data.correct: He lied and got away with it.

data.incorrect: I want to get my ideas by.
data.correct: I want to get my ideas across.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con TAKE

Otro verbo que cambia de mil formas. Aquí tienes los 5 más usados con "Take".

1.  **Take off:** Despegar (un avión) o Quitarse (ropa).
    *   *The plane takes off at 5 PM.*
    *   *Take off your shoes.*
2.  **Take after:** Parecerse a alguien de la familia (en personalidad o apariencia).
    *   *He takes after his father. They are both stubborn.*
3.  **Take up:** Empezar un nuevo hobby o actividad.
    *   *I want to take up painting.*
4.  **Take over:** Tomar el control de algo (una empresa, una situación).
    *   *The new boss will take over tomorrow.*
5.  **Take back:** Devolver algo (a la tienda) o Retirar lo dicho.
    *   *I take back what I said.*

---
¡Practica o no te acordarás de ellos!

data.incorrect: She takes over her mother.
data.correct: She takes after her mother.

data.incorrect: Take your hat after.
data.correct: Take your hat off.

data.incorrect: I take up my words.
data.correct: I take back my words.

data.incorrect: John is very funny, he takes off his uncle.
data.correct: John is very funny, he takes after his uncle.

data.incorrect: Who will take up when the manager leaves?
data.correct: Who will take over when the manager leaves?

content.explanation:
### Cláusulas Nominales (Noun Clauses)

Una *Noun Clause* es una oración completa (tiene su propio sujeto y verbo) que funciona como si fuera un solo sustantivo (un objeto o sujeto gigante).

Usualmente empiezan con *That, How, What, Where, Why...*

#### Funcionando como Objeto
*   *Sustantivo normal:* I know **the secret**.
*   *Noun Clause:* I know **what you did last summer**. (Toda esa frase es "lo que sé").

#### Funcionando como Sujeto
*   *Sustantivo normal:* **The book** is interesting.
*   *Noun Clause:* **What you said** is interesting. (Toda la frase "Lo que tú dijiste" funciona como el sujeto de "is").

#### Regla de Oro
Dentro de la cláusula nominal, el orden de las palabras es normal (Sujeto + Verbo). **No se usan estructuras de pregunta** (ni do/does/did), aunque empiecen con *What* o *Where*.
*   *Incorrecto:* I understand *why did you leave*.
*   *Correcto:* I understand **why you left**.

---
Construye estos super-sustantivos correctamente.

data.incorrect: What he did are terrible.
data.correct: What he did is terrible.

exercises.instruction: Estructura de afirmación, no de pregunta.

data.incorrect: She asked me where was the bank.
data.correct: She asked me where the bank was.

exercises.instruction: Falta conjugar el verbo tras quitar el auxiliar.

data.incorrect: I know what time does the train leave.
data.correct: I know what time the train leaves.

data.incorrect: I don't know if or not she will come.
data.correct: I don't know whether she will come.

data.incorrect: Tell me who is that man.
data.correct: Tell me who that man is.

content.explanation:
### Cláusulas de Participio

Las cláusulas de participio son una forma muy elegante y compacta de dar información sin usar conjunciones (como *because, when, after*).

Se construyen usando un verbo en **-ING** (present participle) o en **-ED / 3ra columna** (past participle) al principio de la oración.

#### Verbos en -ING (Voz Activa)
Significan "Mientras hacía esto..." o "Dado que hacía esto...".
*   *Normal:* Because he felt sick, he went home.
*   *Participle:* **Feeling sick**, he went home.
*   *Normal:* While I was walking down the street, I saw a cat.
*   *Participle:* **Walking down the street**, I saw a cat.

#### Verbos en Participio Pasado (Voz Pasiva)
Significan "Habiendo sido afectado por esto...".
*   *Normal:* Because it was painted red, the house was visible.
*   *Participle:* **Painted red**, the house was visible.

**ADVERTENCIA (Dangling Participle):** El sujeto de la primera parte DEBE SER el mismo sujeto que el de la segunda parte.
*   *Correcto:* Waiting for the bus, **I** saw a crash. (Yo esperaba, yo vi).
*   *Incorrecto:* Waiting for the bus, **the rain** started. (Parece que la lluvia estaba esperando el bus).

---
Practica esta forma literaria.

exercises.instruction: Dangling participle (El perro no iba caminando al trabajo).

data.incorrect: Walking to work, a dog bit me.
data.correct: Walking to work, I was bitten by a dog.

data.incorrect: Building in 1990, the house is old.
data.correct: Built in 1990, the house is old.

data.incorrect: Knowing not what to do, I cried.
data.correct: Not knowing what to do, I cried.

data.incorrect: Locating in Paris, the museum is famous.
data.correct: Located in Paris, the museum is famous.

data.incorrect: Looking out the window, the trees were green.
data.correct: Looking out the window, I saw the green trees.

content.explanation:
### Adjetivos Compuestos

Puedes crear adjetivos uniendo dos o más palabras con un guion (-). Es una forma muy común de describir algo detalladamente sin usar oraciones largas.

#### Regla de Oro: NUNCA se pluralizan
Cuando usas un sustantivo dentro de un adjetivo compuesto, **pierde su plural** (no lleva 's'), sin importar el número.
*   *Correcto:* A **ten-year-old** boy. (Un niño de diez años).
*   *Incorrecto:* A ten-years-old boy.
*   *Correcto:* A **two-week** holiday. (Unas vacaciones de dos semanas).
*   *Incorrecto:* A two-weeks holiday.

#### Compuestos con Participios (-ing / -ed)
Se unen adverbios o sustantivos con verbos.
*   **Well-known:** Muy conocido (Famoso).
*   **Good-looking:** De buen ver (Atractivo).
*   **English-speaking:** Que habla inglés (*An English-speaking country*).
*   **Sun-dried:** Secado al sol (*Sun-dried tomatoes*).

---
No pongas 's' donde no va en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: I read a five-pages report.
data.correct: I read a five-page report.

data.incorrect: He is a good-known actor.
data.correct: He is a well-known actor.

data.incorrect: I found a twenty-dollars bill.
data.correct: I found a twenty-dollar bill.

data.incorrect: These are hands-made shoes.
data.correct: These are hand-made shoes.

data.incorrect: He is an open-minds person.
data.correct: He is an open-minded person.

content.explanation:
### Artículos con Instituciones (School, Prison, Hospital)

En inglés británico (y a veces americano), el uso de **"The"** antes de instituciones como el hospital, la prisión o la escuela cambia completamente la historia.

#### Sin 'The' (Como paciente/estudiante/preso)
Si usas la institución para el propósito original por el que fue construida, **no usas The**.
*   *Example:* He is in **prison**. (Él es un criminal cumpliendo condena).
*   *Example:* She goes to **school**. (Ella es una estudiante).
*   *Example:* John is in **hospital**. (John está enfermo/internado).

#### Con 'The' (Como visitante)
Si vas a ese lugar de visita o por otra razón (eres un invitado, un trabajador externo, etc.), **SÍ usas The**.
*   *Example:* I went to **the prison** to visit my brother. (Tú eres libre, solo fuiste de visita).
*   *Example:* The mother went to **the school** to speak to the teacher. (La madre no estudia ahí).

---
Presta atención al contexto en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: The thief was sent to the prison.
data.correct: The thief was sent to prison.

data.incorrect: He had a heart attack and is in the hospital.
data.correct: He had a heart attack and is in hospital.

data.incorrect: She studies at the university.
data.correct: She studies at university.

exercises.instruction: Ir a la cama a dormir.

data.incorrect: I am tired, I will go to the bed.
data.correct: I am tired, I will go to bed.

exercises.instruction: El edificio de la iglesia (Para turismo).

data.incorrect: We took a photo of church.
data.correct: We took a photo of the church.

title: Unit 67: In vs On vs At (Place)

content.explanation:
### Preposiciones de Lugar (Matices)

In, On y At se traducen todas como "En". Pero en inglés su uso es muy específico.

#### AT (Puntos específicos y lugares públicos)
Se usa para un punto en el mapa o la puerta de un lugar.
*   *Example:* He is waiting **at** the bus stop. (Punto específico).
*   *Example:* I am **at** the door.

#### ON (Superficies y líneas)
Se usa si estás tocando la superficie de algo, o en una línea (calle/río).
*   *Example:* The book is **on** the table. (Superficie).
*   *Example:* London is **on** the Thames. (En el río).

#### IN (Espacios cerrados 3D)
Se usa si estás dentro de una caja, habitación, o límites (ciudades, países).
*   *Example:* The keys are **in** my pocket.
*   *Example:* I live **in** New York.

*El matiz del rincón:*
*   **In the corner:** En la esquina (adentro de la habitación).
*   **At/On the corner:** En la esquina (afuera en la calle).

---
No te pierdas en el espacio con estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: Somebody is in the door.
data.correct: Somebody is at the door.

data.incorrect: The shop is in Oxford Street.
data.correct: The shop is on Oxford Street.

data.incorrect: My phone is on my pocket.
data.correct: My phone is in my pocket.

data.incorrect: The TV is at the corner of the room.
data.correct: The TV is in the corner of the room.

data.incorrect: Look at the stars on the sky.
data.correct: Look at the stars in the sky.

title: Unit 68: In Time vs On Time

content.explanation:
### Preposiciones de Tiempo (Matices)

Seguimos con In, On y At, pero esta vez hablando del tiempo.

#### Las reglas básicas
*   **At:** Horas exactas (*At 5 PM*), festividades (*At Christmas*).
*   **On:** Días de la semana (*On Monday*) y fechas específicas (*On May 4th*).
*   **In:** Meses, años y siglos (*In 2020, In December*).

#### In Time vs On Time (El matiz importante)
*   **On time:** A la hora en punto, puntual. Ni un minuto tarde ni uno temprano, a la hora acordada.
    *   *Example:* The 8:00 train arrived exactly **on time**.
*   **In time:** A tiempo para hacer algo o antes de que sea demasiado tarde (usualmente con margen de sobra).
    *   *Example:* I arrived at the station **in time** to buy a coffee before the train left.

*Truco:* Usamos "Just in time" para decir "justo a tiempo" (salvado por la campana).

---
No llegues tarde a estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: I will see you in Friday.
data.correct: I will see you on Friday.

data.incorrect: My birthday is in May 5th.
data.correct: My birthday is on May 5th.

data.incorrect: Will you be home on time for dinner?
data.correct: Will you be home in time for dinner?

data.incorrect: We always ski in Christmas.
data.correct: We always ski at Christmas.

data.incorrect: The meeting started right in time.
data.correct: The meeting started right on time.

content.explanation:
### El Modo Subjuntivo (Súper Formal)

El subjuntivo en inglés es muy sutil y solo se usa en situaciones formales o legales. Se usa después de verbos que expresan **importancia, sugerencia o exigencia** (suggest, recommend, demand, insist).

#### La Regla de Oro (Verbo Base)
En la oración que sigue al "that", el verbo **siempre va en su forma base original**, sin importar el sujeto o el tiempo. ¡No lleva 's' para he/she/it! ¡Y el verbo To Be es simplemente "be"!

*   *Normal:* I suggest that he **goes** home. (Informal/incorrecto gramaticalmente).
*   *Subjuntivo:* I suggest that he **go** home. (Correcto formalmente. Nota que no hay 'es').

*   *Normal:* It is essential that she **is** here.
*   *Subjuntivo:* It is essential that she **be** here.

#### El Negativo
Para hacer el negativo, solo pon **"not"** antes del verbo base (no uses don't/doesn't).
*   *Example:* I demanded that he **not speak**.

---
Demuestra tu nivel C1/C2 en estos ejercicios.

exercises.instruction: Forma base de To Be es 'be'.

data.incorrect: It is important that she is told.
data.correct: It is important that she be told.

data.incorrect: We request that she doesn't smoke here.
data.correct: We request that she not smoke here.

exercises.instruction: No se conjuga en pasado la segunda parte.

data.incorrect: I insisted that he apologized.
data.correct: I insisted that he apologize.

data.incorrect: The doctor suggested that John loses weight.
data.correct: The doctor suggested that John lose weight.

data.incorrect: It is mandatory that you are on time.
data.correct: It is mandatory that you be on time.

content.explanation:
### Cleft Sentences (Énfasis)

Si quieres enfatizar una parte específica de una oración (quién lo hizo, cuándo, dónde), puedes "partir" la oración (cleft) usando "It is/was" o "What".

*Oración base:* John broke the window yesterday.

#### Énfasis con "It is / It was"
Empiezas con "It was" + la cosa a enfatizar + "who/that" + el resto.
*   **Enfatizar QUIÉN:** **It was John who** broke the window yesterday. (Fue John el que la rompió).
*   **Enfatizar QUÉ:** **It was the window that** John broke yesterday.
*   **Enfatizar CUÁNDO:** **It was yesterday that** John broke the window.

#### Énfasis con "What" (Lo que...)
Empiezas con "What" para enfatizar la acción o el objeto.
*   *Example:* **What** John broke **was** the window. (Lo que John rompió fue la ventana).
*   *Example:* **What** I need **is** a coffee. (Lo que necesito es un café).

---
¡Practica poner el foco donde importa en estos últimos ejercicios del bloque!

data.incorrect: The thing I want is sleep.
data.correct: What I want is sleep.

data.incorrect: It was in 1990 when I was born.
data.correct: It was in 1990 that I was born.

data.incorrect: What he did was to broke it.
data.correct: What he did was break it.

data.incorrect: It was my car which was stolen.
data.correct: It was my car that was stolen.

data.incorrect: It were the dogs who barked.
data.correct: It was the dogs that barked.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con BRING

El verbo "Bring" (Traer) cambia completamente de significado cuando le agregas preposiciones.

Aquí tienes los 5 más importantes:
1.  **Bring up:** Mencionar un tema en una conversación, O criar a un niño.
    *   *Example:* Please don't **bring up** my ex-boyfriend.
    *   *Example:* I was **brought up** in London.
2.  **Bring about:** Provocar o causar que algo suceda.
    *   *Example:* The internet **brought about** huge changes.
3.  **Bring down:** Entristecer a alguien, o derrocar un gobierno/sistema.
    *   *Example:* This sad music is **bringing** me **down**.
4.  **Bring out:** Sacar a la luz, resaltar una cualidad, o lanzar un producto.
    *   *Example:* Stress **brings out** the worst in me.
5.  **Bring back:** Devolver, o hacer recordar (nostalgia).
    *   *Example:* That song **brings back** memories.

---
Practica estos phrasal verbs en contexto.

data.incorrect: She was brought out in Spain.
data.correct: She was brought up in Spain.

data.incorrect: You are bringing me up with that story.
data.correct: You are bringing me down with that story.

data.incorrect: This photo brings down memories.
data.correct: This photo brings back memories.

data.incorrect: The rebels brought up the king.
data.correct: The rebels brought down the king.

data.incorrect: What brought out this crisis?
data.correct: What brought about this crisis?

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con LOOK

El verbo "Look" (Mirar) tiene algunas de las combinaciones más usadas en el día a día.

Aquí tienes las 5 más importantes:
1.  **Look forward to:** Esperar con ansias algo. (¡Atención! Va seguido de sustantivo o verbo en **-ING**).
    *   *Example:* I am **looking forward to seeing** you.
2.  **Look up to:** Admirar o respetar a alguien.
    *   *Example:* I really **look up to** my grandfather.
3.  **Look down on:** Menospreciar a alguien (mirar por encima del hombro).
    *   *Example:* He **looks down on** poor people.
4.  **Look up:** Buscar información (en un diccionario, internet, etc.).
    *   *Example:* I didn't know the word, so I **looked it up**.
5.  **Look after:** Cuidar de alguien o algo.
    *   *Example:* Can you **look after** my dog this weekend?

---
Míralos bien y resuelve los ejercicios.

exercises.instruction: El verbo después de forward to va en -ing.

data.incorrect: I look forward to go home.
data.correct: I look forward to going home.

data.incorrect: Don't look up on him just because he is young.
data.correct: Don't look down on him just because he is young.

data.incorrect: Who will look for the baby?
data.correct: Who will look after the baby?

data.incorrect: I am looking forward the weekend.
data.correct: I am looking forward to the weekend.

data.incorrect: He is my hero, I look after him.
data.correct: He is my hero, I look up to him.

content.explanation:
### Verbos seguidos de Gerundio (-ing)

En inglés, cuando tienes dos verbos juntos (ej: Me encanta correr), el segundo verbo debe ir en infinitivo (to run) o en gerundio (running). ¡Depende totalmente del primer verbo!

Esta es una lista de verbos que **SIEMPRE exigen que el siguiente verbo termine en -ING**.
*   **Enjoy** (Disfrutar): I enjoy **reading**. (Nunca: *I enjoy to read*).
*   **Avoid** (Evitar): He avoided **answering**.
*   **Mind** (Importar/Molestar): I don't mind **waiting**.
*   **Finish** (Terminar): Have you finished **eating**?
*   **Suggest** (Sugerir): I suggest **leaving** now.
*   **Keep** (Mantener/Seguir): Keep **going**!
*   **Consider** (Considerar): We considered **moving** to Paris.

*(Consejo: Memoriza esta lista. No hay una regla lógica, simplemente es así).*

---
Aplica la regla del -ING en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: You should avoid to eat junk food.
data.correct: You should avoid eating junk food.

data.incorrect: I haven't finished to work.
data.correct: I haven't finished working.

data.incorrect: Just keep to swim.
data.correct: Just keep swimming.

data.incorrect: He denied to steal the money.
data.correct: He denied stealing the money.

data.incorrect: I miss to see my friends.
data.correct: I miss seeing my friends.

content.explanation:
### Verbos seguidos de Infinitivo (To Do)

A diferencia de la unidad anterior, esta es la lista de verbos que **SIEMPRE exigen que el siguiente verbo lleve "TO" (infinitivo)**.

*   **Want** (Querer): I want **to go**. (Nunca: *I want going*).
*   **Decide** (Decidir): We decided **to leave**.
*   **Hope** (Esperar con ilusión): I hope **to see** you.
*   **Plan** (Planear): They plan **to travel**.
*   **Agree** (Acordar): He agreed **to help**.
*   **Promise** (Prometer): She promised **to call**.
*   **Learn** (Aprender): I am learning **to drive**.

*(Si intentas poner un verbo en -ING después de estos verbos, sonarás muy poco natural).*

---
Coloca el "TO" donde corresponde en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: We decided staying at home.
data.correct: We decided to stay at home.

data.incorrect: I am planning moving to London.
data.correct: I am planning to move to London.

data.incorrect: She promised calling me later.
data.correct: She promised to call me later.

data.incorrect: He offered helping me.
data.correct: He offered to help me.

data.incorrect: He seems being tired.
data.correct: He seems to be tired.

content.explanation:
### Verbos que cambian de significado (-ing vs To)

¡Aquí es donde se complica! Algunos verbos aceptan tanto **-ING** como **TO**, pero el significado de la frase cambia por completo.

#### STOP
*   **Stop + ING:** Dejar de hacer el hábito. *I stopped smoking.* (Dejé el cigarro).
*   **Stop + TO:** Detenerte físicamente PARA hacer algo. *I stopped to smoke.* (Paré el coche para fumar).

#### REMEMBER (Recordar)
*   **Remember + ING:** Recuerdos del pasado. *I remember locking the door.* (Tengo el recuerdo en mi mente).
*   **Remember + TO:** Una tarea pendiente. *Remember to lock the door!* (¡No olvides hacerlo!).

#### FORGET (Olvidar)
*   **Forget + ING:** Olvidar una memoria. *I will never forget meeting you.* (Nunca olvidaré el recuerdo).
*   **Forget + TO:** Olvidar hacer una tarea. *I forgot to buy milk.* (Se me olvidó hacerlo).

---
Presta mucha atención al contexto para elegir bien.

data.incorrect: While driving, we stopped buying coffee.
data.correct: While driving, we stopped to buy coffee.

data.incorrect: Please remember turning off the lights.
data.correct: Please remember to turn off the lights.

data.incorrect: I will never forget to see the ocean.
data.correct: I will never forget seeing the ocean.

data.incorrect: Don't forget bringing your passport.
data.correct: Don't forget to bring your passport.

data.incorrect: I saw John and stopped talking to him.
data.correct: I saw John and stopped to talk to him.

content.explanation:
### Inversión para dar Énfasis (Nivel C2)

Cuando queremos ser súper dramáticos o formales (especialmente al escribir), podemos poner adverbios negativos al principio de la oración (*Never, Rarely, Seldom, Not only*).

Pero si haces esto, ¡la gramática enloquece! Tienes que **invertir el sujeto y el verbo**, poniéndolos en forma de pregunta (Auxiliar + Sujeto + Verbo), aunque NO sea una pregunta.

#### Ejemplos con Never y Rarely
*   *Normal:* I have never seen such a beautiful sunset.
*   *Inversión:* **Never have I seen** such a beautiful sunset. (¡Nunca había visto!).
*   *Normal:* He rarely speaks to me.
*   *Inversión:* **Rarely does he speak** to me. (Se usa 'does' como si fuera pregunta).

#### Not only... but also (No solo... sino también)
Solo se invierte la primera parte.
*   *Normal:* He is not only rich, but he is also handsome.
*   *Inversión:* **Not only is he** rich, but he is also handsome.

---
Practica esta estructura poética y dramática.

data.incorrect: Rarely she goes to the cinema.
data.correct: Rarely does she go to the cinema.

data.incorrect: Never I felt so happy.
data.correct: Never did I feel so happy.

data.incorrect: No sooner I arrived than he left.
data.correct: No sooner had I arrived than he left.

exercises.instruction: Little did I know (Poco sabía yo).

data.incorrect: Under no circumstances you should open the door.
data.correct: Under no circumstances should you open the door.

exercises.instruction: Falta el verbo base tras el did.

data.incorrect: Never did I saw such a thing.
data.correct: Never did I see such a thing.

content.explanation:
### Condicionales Mixtos

¿Qué pasa cuando una decisión del pasado afecta tu presente? Para eso están los condicionales mixtos, mezclan el 3er condicional (pasado) con el 2do condicional (presente).

#### Mezcla 1: Condición pasada -> Resultado presente
Usas **Past Perfect (had + V3)** en el 'If', y **Would + Verbo base** para el resultado.
*   *Realidad:* I didn't study yesterday, so I am failing the test now.
*   *Condicional:* If I **had studied** yesterday, I **would pass** the test now. (Si hubiera estudiado, pasaría el examen).
*   *Otro:* If I **had won** the lottery, I **would be** rich now.

#### Mezcla 2: Situación permanente -> Resultado pasado
Usas **Past Simple** en el 'If' (porque es una verdad general), y **Would have + V3** para el resultado.
*   *Realidad:* I am short, so I didn't play basketball in college.
*   *Condicional:* If I **were** tall, I **would have played** basketball in college. (Si yo fuera alto, habría jugado).

---
No te pierdas en el tiempo.

data.incorrect: If I had eaten breakfast, I would have not be hungry now.
data.correct: If I had eaten breakfast, I wouldn't be hungry now.

exercises.instruction: Si hubiera nacido allí (pasado), hablaría fluído (presente).

data.incorrect: If I was born there, I would speak fluent English.
data.correct: If I had been born there, I would speak fluent English.

exercises.instruction: Si no te hubiera conocido (pasado) -> estaría solo (presente).
Verdad permanente (No tener miedo) afecta evento pasado.

data.incorrect: If I hadn't been afraid of heights, I would jump out of the plane yesterday.
data.correct: If I wasn't afraid of heights, I would have jumped out of the plane yesterday.

exercises.instruction: Si fuera francés (verdad), habría entendido el chiste (pasado).

data.incorrect: If I had been French, I would understand the joke.
data.correct: If I were French, I would have understood the joke.

exercises.instruction: Si no hubiera gastado el dinero.
Uso de 'were' para To Be en condicionales.

data.incorrect: If I was you, I would have told him the truth.
data.correct: If I were you, I would have told him the truth.

content.explanation:
### Cláusulas Relativas (El uso de la coma)

Usamos *who, which, that* para dar más información sobre un sustantivo. Pero ¿cuándo llevan comas?

#### Defining Clauses (Sin comas)
La información es **esencial**. Si la quitas, no sabes de quién se está hablando. Puedes usar "That" en lugar de "Who" o "Which".
*   *Example:* The man **who stole my car** has been arrested. (Si quitas la cláusula, queda "El hombre fue arrestado". ¿Cuál hombre? No se entiende. Por lo tanto, NO hay comas).
*   *Example:* The car **that** I bought is red.

#### Non-Defining Clauses (Con comas)
La información es **extra** (solo un detalle adicional). Si la quitas, la frase sigue teniendo perfecto sentido. ¡NUNCA uses "That" aquí!
*   *Example:* John**, who is 40 years old,** has been arrested. (Si lo quitas, dice "John fue arrestado". Sabemos perfectamente quién es John. La edad es solo un detalle).
*   *Incorrecto:* John, *that* is 40 years old...

---
Presta atención a la información esencial en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: My father, that is a doctor, is retiring.
data.correct: My father, who is a doctor, is retiring.

exercises.instruction: La Torre Eiffel es única (lleva comas).

data.incorrect: The Eiffel Tower which is in Paris is very tall.
data.correct: The Eiffel Tower, which is in Paris, is very tall.

data.incorrect: My brother, works in a bank, is rich.
data.correct: My brother, who works in a bank, is rich.

exercises.instruction: No se repite el pronombre objeto.

data.incorrect: The man who I met him yesterday is my boss.
data.correct: The man who I met yesterday is my boss.

exercises.instruction: Omitir el pronombre (solo posible si NO es el sujeto).

data.incorrect: The man stole my bag ran away.
data.correct: The man who stole my bag ran away.

content.explanation:
### Frases Preposicionales Comunes

Muchas veces las preposiciones no siguen reglas lógicas, simplemente tienes que aprenderte las combinaciones fijas (collocations).

Aquí tienes algunas esenciales para sonar natural:
*   **By mistake:** Por error (sin querer). *I sent the email by mistake.*
*   **On purpose:** A propósito (intencionalmente). *He broke it on purpose.*
*   **In charge of:** A cargo de (responsable de). *She is in charge of sales.*
*   **Out of date:** Obsoleto o vencido. *This passport is out of date.*
*   **Up to date:** Actualizado. *Keep me up to date.*
*   **At least:** Al menos. *At least you tried.*
*   **In fact:** De hecho. *It's not cold. In fact, it's hot.*

---
Llena los espacios correctamente con estas frases hechas.

data.incorrect: Did you do that for purpose?
data.correct: Did you do that on purpose?

data.incorrect: In least we have food.
data.correct: At least we have food.

data.incorrect: He is rich. On fact, he is a millionaire.
data.correct: He is rich. In fact, he is a millionaire.

data.incorrect: All the sudden, it started raining.
data.correct: All of a sudden, it started raining.

data.incorrect: Off course I will help you.
data.correct: Of course I will help you.

content.explanation:
### Transiciones Avanzadas (Conectores)

Para escribir ensayos, correos formales o sonar extremadamente educado, debes sustituir "and", "but" y "so" por transiciones de alto nivel. Siempre se separan con comas.

#### En lugar de "And" (Para agregar información)
*   **Furthermore / Moreover:** Además / Por otra parte. *The house is beautiful. Furthermore, it is cheap.*

#### En lugar de "But" (Para contrastar)
*   **Nevertheless / Nonetheless:** Sin embargo / No obstante. *It was raining. Nevertheless, we played football.*

#### En lugar de "So" (Para mostrar resultados)
*   **Consequently / Therefore:** Por lo tanto / Como consecuencia. *He didn't study. Consequently, he failed.*

#### En lugar de "Because of" (Causa formal)
*   **Due to:** Debido a. *The flight was delayed due to the weather.*

---
Eleva tu nivel de escritura con estos ejercicios finales del bloque.

data.incorrect: He is rich nevertheless he is sad.
data.correct: He is rich. Nevertheless, he is sad.

data.incorrect: We canceled the game because to rain.
data.correct: We canceled the game due to rain.

data.incorrect: The car is expensive. Further, it is slow.
data.correct: The car is expensive. Furthermore, it is slow.

exercises.instruction: Por otra parte (On the other hand).
A pesar de esto (In spite of this).

data.incorrect: In spite this, we won.
data.correct: In spite of this, we won.

data.incorrect: In conclude, it was a good year.
data.correct: In conclusion, it was a good year.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con GO

El verbo "Go" es básico, pero con preposiciones cambia drásticamente.

1.  **Go on:** Continuar, o suceder.
    *   *Example:* Please, **go on** with your story.
    *   *Example:* What is **going on** here? (¿Qué está sucediendo?).
2.  **Go off:** Sonar (una alarma/bomba) o echarse a perder (comida).
    *   *Example:* My alarm **went off** at 6 AM.
    *   *Example:* This milk has **gone off**.
3.  **Go over:** Revisar algo detalladamente.
    *   *Example:* Let's **go over** the plan one more time.
4.  **Go against:** Ir en contra de (tus principios, reglas, alguien).
    *   *Example:* Lying **goes against** my rules.
5.  **Go without:** Prescindir de algo (vivir sin ello).
    *   *Example:* A camel can **go without** water for days.

---
Domina estos modismos con GO.

data.incorrect: The alarm went on at midnight.
data.correct: The alarm went off at midnight.

data.incorrect: What is going off here?
data.correct: What is going on here?

data.incorrect: Don't drink that, it went down.
data.correct: Don't drink that, it went off.

data.incorrect: He went over talking for hours.
data.correct: He went on talking for hours.

data.incorrect: The bomb went out.
data.correct: The bomb went off.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con COME

El verbo "Come" (Venir) también esconde grandes secretos.

1.  **Come across:** Encontrarse con alguien o algo por casualidad.
    *   *Example:* I **came across** an old photo today.
2.  **Come up with:** Idear / inventar (una idea, una solución o una excusa).
    *   *Example:* We need to **come up with** a plan.
3.  **Come down with:** Enfermarse de algo (usualmente leve).
    *   *Example:* I think I'm **coming down with** a cold.
4.  **Come off:** Tener éxito (un plan) o desprenderse (una pieza).
    *   *Example:* The handle of the door **came off**.
5.  **Come around:** Cambiar de opinión eventualmente.
    *   *Example:* He hates the idea, but he'll **come around**.

---
Domínalos con estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: I am coming up with a flu.
data.correct: I am coming down with a flu.

data.incorrect: The button came down my shirt.
data.correct: The button came off my shirt.

data.incorrect: He came down with a bad excuse.
data.correct: He came up with a bad excuse.

data.incorrect: The party came over perfectly.
data.correct: The party came off perfectly.

data.incorrect: We must come across a solution.
data.correct: We must come up with a solution.

content.explanation:
### Verbos Causativos (Hacer que alguien haga algo)

A veces tú no haces la acción, sino que *haces que otra persona la haga* por ti (porque le pagas, le obligas o la convences).

#### HAVE (Darle la responsabilidad a alguien)
Estructura: **Have + Persona + VERBO BASE**.
*   *Example:* I **had** the mechanic **fix** my car. (Le encargué al mecánico que lo arreglara).
*   *Incorrecto:* I had the mechanic *to fix*...

#### MAKE (Obligar a alguien)
Estructura: **Make + Persona + VERBO BASE**.
*   *Example:* My mom **made** me **clean** my room. (Me obligó a limpiar).

#### GET (Convencer a alguien)
¡Cuidado! Este es el único que lleva "TO".
Estructura: **Get + Persona + TO + Verbo base**.
*   *Example:* I **got** my brother **to help** me. (Convencí a mi hermano para que me ayudara).

---
¡Haz que alguien más haga el trabajo en estos ejercicios!

data.incorrect: She made me to cry.
data.correct: She made me cry.

data.incorrect: I will have John to call you.
data.correct: I will have John call you.

data.incorrect: How did you get her agree?
data.correct: How did you get her to agree?

data.incorrect: They made him to sign the contract.
data.correct: They made him sign the contract.

data.incorrect: I will have my lawyer to read it.
data.correct: I will have my lawyer read it.

content.explanation:
### Causativo Pasivo (Hacer que te hagan algo)

¿Qué pasa si te cortas el pelo? En español decimos "Me corté el pelo". Pero si dices *I cut my hair* en inglés, significa que tomaste unas tijeras y te lo cortaste tú mismo frente al espejo.

Para decir que pagaste/pediste a alguien más que le hiciera algo a un objeto tuyo, usas el Causativo Pasivo.

#### Estructura: Have/Get + Objeto + VERBO EN PARTICIPIO (V3)
El sujeto (tú) recibe el servicio. El que hace la acción no importa.

*   *Example:* I **had my car fixed**. (Hice que me arreglaran el coche).
*   *Example:* I **am getting my hair cut**. (Me están cortando el pelo).
*   *Example:* She **has had her house painted**. (A ella le han pintado la casa).

*Nota: Have y Get significan lo mismo aquí, pero Get es un poco más informal.*

---
¡No hagas el trabajo tú mismo en estos ejercicios!

exercises.instruction: Tú no te cortas el pelo solo.

data.incorrect: I cut my hair yesterday at the salon.
data.correct: I had my hair cut yesterday at the salon.

data.incorrect: I had my watch repair.
data.correct: I had my watch repaired.

data.incorrect: I got my computer fixing.
data.correct: I got my computer fixed.

data.incorrect: I need to get my suit clean.
data.correct: I need to get my suit cleaned.

data.incorrect: I must test my eyes.
data.correct: I must have my eyes tested.

content.explanation:
### Preguntas Integradas (Embedded Questions)

Para sonar súper educado en inglés, no hacemos preguntas directas. Las "integramos" dentro de otra frase como *Do you know...?, Can you tell me...?, I wonder...*

#### La Regla de Oro
Cuando haces esto, **la segunda parte pierde su estructura de pregunta**. Ya no usas auxiliares (do/does/did) y el orden vuelve a ser Afirmativo (Sujeto + Verbo).

*   *Directa:* **Where is** the bank? (Verbo + Sujeto).
*   *Embedded:* Do you know **where the bank is**? (Sujeto + Verbo). ¡El verbo To Be va al final!

*   *Directa:* **What time does** the train leave?
*   *Embedded:* Can you tell me **what time the train leaves**? (El "does" desaparece y el verbo lleva 's').

#### Preguntas de Sí o No
Si la pregunta directa se responde con Sí/No, usamos **"if"** o **"whether"** para integrarla.
*   *Directa:* Is he married?
*   *Embedded:* Do you know **if he is** married?

---
Sé muy educado y cuidadoso con el orden en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: Do you know what does this mean?
data.correct: Do you know what this means?

data.incorrect: I wonder where did they go.
data.correct: I wonder where they went.

data.incorrect: Can you tell me what time is it?
data.correct: Can you tell me what time it is?

data.incorrect: Do you know how do you do this?
data.correct: Do you know how you do this?

data.incorrect: I am not sure how much does it cost.
data.correct: I am not sure how much it costs.

content.explanation:
### El Orden de los Adjetivos

En español podemos poner los adjetivos casi en cualquier orden. Pero en inglés, si usas varios adjetivos para describir algo, hay una regla no escrita (OSASCOMP) que los nativos siguen instintivamente.

Si los pones en otro orden, sonarás muy raro.

#### La Regla OSASCOMP
1.  **O**pinion (Opinión: beautiful, ugly, smart)
2.  **S**ize (Tamaño: big, small, tall)
3.  **A**ge (Edad: old, new, young)
4.  **S**hape (Forma: round, square)
5.  **C**olor (Color: red, blue)
6.  **O**rigin (Origen/Nacionalidad: Spanish, Italian)
7.  **M**aterial (Material: wooden, plastic, metal)
8.  **P**urpose (Propósito: sleeping (bag), running (shoes))

*   *Example:* A **beautiful** (opinión), **small** (tamaño), **old** (edad), **red** (color), **Italian** (origen), **wooden** (material) box.

---
Ordena tus ideas y tus adjetivos en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: I bought a red big car.
data.correct: I bought a big red car.

data.incorrect: He is a French nice man.
data.correct: He is a nice French man.

data.incorrect: I need my running new shoes.
data.correct: I need my new running shoes.

data.incorrect: It is a green beautiful small bird.
data.correct: It is a beautiful small green bird.

data.incorrect: A plastic ugly chair.
data.correct: An ugly plastic chair.

content.explanation:
### Adjetivos con -ED vs -ING

Confundir estos dos puede cambiar totalmente lo que quieres decir. ¿Eres aburrido o estás aburrido?

#### Adjetivos con -ED (El Sentimiento)
Describen **cómo se siente alguien**. (Solo los humanos y animales tienen sentimientos).
*   *Example:* I am **bored**. (Estoy aburrido/Me siento aburrido).
*   *Example:* She is **interested**. (Ella está interesada).

#### Adjetivos con -ING (La Causa)
Describen a la persona, cosa o situación **que causa el sentimiento**.
*   *Example:* The movie is **boring**. (La película causa aburrimiento).
*   *Example:* John is a **boring** person. (John es aburrido, causa que yo me sienta 'bored').
*   *Example:* This book is **interesting**. (El libro causa interés).

---
Cuidado con decir que eres "aburrido" cuando quieres decir que "estás aburrido" en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: My job is very tired.
data.correct: My job is very tiring.

data.incorrect: The news was very surprised.
data.correct: The news was very surprising.

data.incorrect: I am very confusing right now.
data.correct: I am very confused right now.

data.incorrect: The game was very excited.
data.correct: The game was very exciting.

data.incorrect: That noise is very annoyed.
data.correct: That noise is very annoying.

content.explanation:
### Future Perfect Continuous

Este es el último y más largo de los tiempos verbales en inglés. Se usa para proyectarte hacia el futuro y mirar hacia atrás para decir **cuánto tiempo llevará ocurriendo una acción continua**.

#### Estructura
**Sujeto + Will + Have + Been + Verbo-ing** (Habré estado haciendo...)

*   *Example:* By December, I **will have been working** here for ten years. (Para diciembre, habré estado trabajando aquí por 10 años).
*   *Example:* By 8 PM, we **will have been driving** for six hours. (Habremos estado conduciendo).

Se usa casi siempre con **By** (Para cuando...) y **For** (por X cantidad de tiempo).

---
Construye esta estructura gigante en los ejercicios.

data.incorrect: By tomorrow, I will have studying for a week.
data.correct: By tomorrow, I will have been studying for a week.

exercises.instruction: Tercera persona no usa 'has' aquí porque está 'will' antes.

data.incorrect: He will has been working.
data.correct: He will have been working.

data.incorrect: I will have been wait for two hours.
data.correct: I will have been waiting for two hours.

data.incorrect: We will are been driving.
data.correct: We will have been driving.

data.incorrect: I will been running for an hour.
data.correct: I will have been running for an hour.

content.explanation:
### Concesión: Although vs Despite

Ya los vimos brevemente antes, pero su estructura causa muchos dolores de cabeza. Sirven para decir "A pesar de que...".

#### Although / Even though / Though
SIEMPRE van seguidos de una oración completa (**Sujeto + Verbo**).
*   *Correcto:* **Although it was** raining, we went out.
*   *Incorrecto:* Although the rain...

#### Despite / In spite of
SIEMPRE van seguidos de un **Sustantivo** o un verbo en **-ING**. ¡NUNCA Sujeto + Verbo!
*   *Correcto:* **Despite the rain**, we went out.
*   *Correcto:* **In spite of being** sick, I worked.
*   *Incorrecto:* Despite it was raining...

#### El truco: "the fact that"
Si realmente necesitas usar un Sujeto + Verbo después de Despite, tienes que agregar la frase "the fact that".
*   *Example:* **Despite the fact that** it was raining, we went out.

---
Elige la estructura correcta en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: Despite he was tired, he kept going.
data.correct: Despite being tired, he kept going.

data.incorrect: Despite that he was sick, he worked.
data.correct: Despite the fact that he was sick, he worked.

exercises.instruction: Although no va con solo sustantivo.

data.incorrect: Although the rain, we won.
data.correct: Despite the rain, we won.

data.incorrect: Despite of the traffic, we arrived.
data.correct: Despite the traffic, we arrived.

data.incorrect: In spite of she was crying, she smiled.
data.correct: Although she was crying, she smiled.

content.explanation:
### Condicionales: Unless vs As long as

Estas dos frases son sustitutos para "If" y "If not", y hacen que tu inglés suene mucho más avanzado.

#### Unless (A menos que / Si no)
Significa "If not" y es inherentemente negativo. Por lo tanto, **el verbo que le sigue debe ir en afirmativo** (¡prohibido el doble negativo!).
*   *Normal:* I won't go **if** you do **not** pay me.
*   *Con Unless:* I won't go **unless** you **pay** me. (A menos que me pagues).
*   *Incorrecto:* I won't go unless you *don't* pay me.

#### As long as / Provided that (Siempre y cuando)
Significa que una cosa solo sucederá si se cumple una condición estricta.
*   *Example:* You can go to the party **as long as** you are back by 10 PM. (Siempre y cuando regreses a las 10).
*   *Example:* I will lend you the money **provided that** you pay me back tomorrow.

---
Pon tus condiciones en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: Unless you don't study, you will fail.
data.correct: Unless you study, you will fail.

exercises.instruction: Unless es negativo por sí solo.

data.incorrect: We will miss the train unless we don't hurry.
data.correct: We will miss the train unless we hurry.

data.incorrect: You can drive my car so long you are careful.
data.correct: You can drive my car as long as you are careful.

data.incorrect: I can't open it unless I don't have the key.
data.correct: I can't open it unless I have the key.

data.incorrect: Unless you apologize, I will forgive you.
data.correct: Unless you apologize, I won't forgive you.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con SET

El verbo "Set" por sí solo significa colocar o establecer, pero con preposiciones es muy común en el ámbito laboral y de viajes.

1.  **Set up:** Configurar algo (equipo) o fundar/crear una empresa.
    *   *Example:* I am going to **set up** my own business.
    *   *Example:* Can you help me **set up** this new computer?
2.  **Set off:** Emprender un viaje, o hacer detonar/sonar algo.
    *   *Example:* We will **set off** early tomorrow morning. (Iniciar el viaje).
    *   *Example:* Smoke **set off** the fire alarm.
3.  **Set out:** Tener la intención/objetivo de hacer algo, o exponer algo claramente.
    *   *Example:* He **set out** to become the best player.
4.  **Set back:** Retrasar el progreso de algo, o costar mucho dinero.
    *   *Example:* The storm **set** the project **back** by two weeks.
    *   *Example:* That car must have **set** you **back** a bit!
5.  **Set in:** Establecerse (usualmente algo negativo como mal clima, pánico o infección) y parecer que va a durar.
    *   *Example:* Winter has really **set in** early this year.

---
Configura tu mente y prepárate para los ejercicios.

data.incorrect: We must set up early tomorrow.
data.correct: We must set off early tomorrow.

data.incorrect: The rain set us down by a week.
data.correct: The rain set us back by a week.

data.incorrect: The smoke set out the alarm.
data.correct: The smoke set off the alarm.

data.incorrect: That house set you off millions.
data.correct: That house set you back millions.

data.incorrect: I need to set off my new phone.
data.correct: I need to set up my new phone.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con PUT

El verbo "Put" (Poner) forma decenas de modismos, muchos de ellos relacionados con el tiempo y el esfuerzo.

1.  **Put off:** Posponer algo para después.
    *   *Example:* We have to **put off** the meeting until tomorrow.
2.  **Put up with:** Tolerar o aguantar algo (o a alguien) molesto.
    *   *Example:* I can't **put up with** this noise anymore!
3.  **Put away:** Guardar algo en su lugar (después de usarlo).
    *   *Example:* Please **put away** your toys.
4.  **Put out:** Apagar (un fuego/cigarrillo) o causar molestias a alguien.
    *   *Example:* The firefighters **put out** the fire.
    *   *Example:* I hope I'm not **putting** you **out**.
5.  **Put on:** Ponerse ropa (la acción de vestirse) o ganar peso.
    *   *Example:* It's cold, **put on** your jacket.
    *   *Example:* I **put on** 2 kilos during the holidays.

---
No lo pospongas, haz los ejercicios ahora.

data.incorrect: I can't put on with his attitude.
data.correct: I can't put up with his attitude.

data.incorrect: Put down your books in the closet.
data.correct: Put away your books in the closet.

data.incorrect: Never put out until tomorrow what you can do today.
data.correct: Never put off until tomorrow what you can do today.

data.incorrect: You have to put up the pain.
data.correct: You have to put up with the pain.

data.incorrect: I hope I am not putting you away.
data.correct: I hope I am not putting you out.

content.explanation:
### Modales de Deducción en Pasado

Cuando juegas a ser detective y quieres adivinar qué pasó en el pasado, usas **Must, Can't, y Might** acompañados de **have + V3**.

#### MUST HAVE + V3 (Certeza Positiva)
Estás 99% seguro de que algo ocurrió debido a la evidencia.
*   *Situación:* La calle está mojada.
*   *Deducción:* It **must have rained**. (Debe haber llovido).

#### CAN'T HAVE / COULDN'T HAVE + V3 (Certeza Negativa)
Estás 99% seguro de que algo NO ocurrió porque es imposible. (¡Nunca uses mustn't para esto!).
*   *Situación:* John estaba conmigo a esa hora.
*   *Deducción:* John **can't have robbed** the bank. (John no puede haber robado el banco).

#### MIGHT HAVE / MAY HAVE / COULD HAVE + V3 (Posibilidad)
Crees que quizá sucedió (50% de seguridad).
*   *Situación:* No encuentro mis llaves.
*   *Deducción:* I **might have left** them at home. (Quizá las dejé en casa).

---
Saca tus propias conclusiones en estos ejercicios.

exercises.instruction: Estoy seguro de que él lo hizo (Must).

data.incorrect: He mustn't have stolen the car, he was with me.
data.correct: He can't have stolen the car, he was with me.

data.incorrect: She must forgot the meeting.
data.correct: She must have forgotten the meeting.

data.incorrect: It couldn't has been him.
data.correct: It couldn't have been him.

data.incorrect: It must had been raining.
data.correct: It must have been raining.

data.incorrect: You can't finished already!
data.correct: You can't have finished already!

content.explanation:
### Preferencias: Would Rather vs Prefer

Expresar preferencias tiene sus trampas, especialmente si quieres que *otra persona* haga (o no haga) algo.

#### PREFER (Reglas generales)
Normalmente va seguido de **to + verbo** o un gerundio (**-ing**).
*   *Example:* I prefer **to drink** tea. (Prefiero tomar té).
*   *Example:* I prefer tea **to** coffee. (Comparando dos sustantivos, usa "to").

#### WOULD RATHER (Yo prefiero hacer algo)
Va seguido del **verbo base** (Sin To). Su contracción es **I'd rather**.
*   *Example:* I'd rather **drink** tea.
*   *Incorrecto:* I'd rather *to drink* tea.
*   *Comparando:* I'd rather drink tea **than** coffee. (Usa "than").

#### WOULD RATHER + OTRO SUJETO (Quiero que TÚ hagas algo)
¡Cuidado aquí! Si prefieres que **otra persona** haga algo AHORA o en el FUTURO, el verbo debe ir en **Pasado Simple** (Condicional irreal).
*   *Example:* I'd rather you **went** home now. (Prefiero que TE VAYAS a casa ahora).
*   *Example:* I'd rather you **didn't** smoke here. (Prefiero que no fumes aquí).

---
Elige tus preferencias sabiamente.

exercises.instruction: Preferir que OTRO haga algo (usa Pasado).

data.incorrect: I'd rather you stay here.
data.correct: I'd rather you stayed here.

data.incorrect: I'd rather walk to take the bus.
data.correct: I'd rather walk than take the bus.

exercises.instruction: Preferir que otro NO haga algo.

data.incorrect: We would rather to eat pizza.
data.correct: We would rather eat pizza.

data.incorrect: She prefers reading than watching TV.
data.correct: She prefers reading to watching TV.

data.incorrect: I'd rather you pay today.
data.correct: I'd rather you paid today.

content.explanation:
### 'IT' como Objeto Preparatorio

En español es común decir: "Me resulta difícil creer eso". 
En inglés, no puedes decir "I find difficult to believe that". Los verbos siempre necesitan un objeto directo.

Para solucionar esto, metemos un objeto falso (**IT**) y dejamos la acción real para el final de la oración.

#### Verbos comunes que usan esta estructura
*Find, Think, Make, Consider, Believe*.

#### Estructura
**Sujeto + Verbo + IT + Adjetivo + TO (Verbo real).**

*   *Example:* I find **it** difficult **to learn** Russian. (Encuentro "eso" difícil: aprender ruso).
*   *Example:* The internet makes **it** possible **to work** from home. (Internet hace "eso" posible: trabajar).
*   *Example:* He considers **it** a privilege **to be** here.

---
No olvides tu objeto falso en los ejercicios.

data.incorrect: The car makes easy to travel.
data.correct: The car makes it easy to travel.

exercises.instruction: Falta el TO en la acción real.

data.incorrect: I find it impossible sleep here.
data.correct: I find it impossible to sleep here.

exercises.instruction: Cuidado con poner 'that' en vez de 'it'.

data.incorrect: I think that essential to know the rules.
data.correct: I think it essential to know the rules.

exercises.instruction: El adjetivo va antes del verbo real.

data.incorrect: I made it to finish early possible.
data.correct: I made it possible to finish early.

data.incorrect: He found difficult believing her.
data.correct: He found it difficult to believe her.

content.explanation:
### Voz Pasiva Avanzada (Impersonal)

Normalmente, la voz pasiva se usa para enfocar el objeto (El coche fue robado). Pero hay una forma avanzada que se usa en **noticias y rumores** cuando no sabemos o no nos importa quién dijo algo.

Se usa con verbos de reporte: *say, think, believe, consider, report*.

#### Forma 1: "It is said that..."
Usas un "It" falso.
*   *Rumor:* People say that he is rich.
*   *Pasiva Formal:* **It is said that** he is rich. (Se dice que él es rico).
*   *Pasado:* **It was believed that** the earth was flat. (Se creía que...).

#### Forma 2: "He is said to..." (Súper Avanzado)
El sujeto del rumor se convierte en el sujeto de la frase pasiva, seguido del **verbo en infinitivo (to + base)**.
*   *Rumor:* People say that he is rich.
*   *Pasiva Avanzada:* **He is said to be** rich. (Se dice de él, que es rico).
*   *Ejemplo de noticia:* The thief **is believed to live** in London.

---
Domina el lenguaje del periodismo en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: He is thought be a spy.
data.correct: He is thought to be a spy.

data.incorrect: It believed that they escaped.
data.correct: It was believed that they escaped.

data.incorrect: He is said to stole it.
data.correct: He is said to have stolen it.

data.incorrect: They are consider to be dangerous.
data.correct: They are considered to be dangerous.

data.incorrect: It said that coffee is good.
data.correct: It is said that coffee is good.

content.explanation:
### Verbos de Reporte (Reported Speech avanzado)

En vez de decir *He said "I won't do it"*, es más natural resumir la intención usando verbos específicos como negar, prometer, sugerir o disculparse. El problema es que **cada verbo exige una estructura diferente**.

#### Verbos seguidos de -ING
*   **Deny (Negar):** He denied **stealing** the money.
*   **Admit (Admitir):** She admitted **lying**.
*   **Suggest (Sugerir):** I suggested **leaving** early.

#### Verbos seguidos de TO + Infinitivo
*   **Promise (Prometer):** He promised **to call**.
*   **Offer (Ofrecerse):** She offered **to help**.
*   **Refuse (Rehusarse):** They refused **to pay**.

#### Verbos seguidos de Preposición + -ING
*   **Apologize for (Disculparse por):** He apologized **for being** late.
*   **Accuse of (Acusar de):** They accused him **of stealing**.

---
Escoge el camino correcto para cada verbo.

data.incorrect: She denied to break the glass.
data.correct: She denied breaking the glass.

data.incorrect: He suggested to eat pizza.
data.correct: He suggested eating pizza.

data.incorrect: He apologized to be late.
data.correct: He apologized for being late.

data.incorrect: He offered helping with the bags.
data.correct: He offered to help with the bags.

data.incorrect: He insisted to pay the bill.
data.correct: He insisted on paying the bill.

content.explanation:
### Artículos: Nombres Abstractos

El artículo "The" (El, La, Los, Las) causa muchos problemas a los hispanohablantes porque en español lo usamos para todo.

#### La Regla de los Conceptos Abstractos
**NUNCA uses "The" cuando hablas de un concepto general o abstracto** (Amor, Vida, Tiempo, Dinero, Música, Historia).
*   *Correcto:* **Love** is blind. (El amor es ciego).
*   *Incorrecto:* *The love* is blind.
*   *Correcto:* **Life** is hard. (La vida es dura).
*   *Incorrecto:* *The life* is hard.

#### ¿Cuándo SÍ usamos 'The'?
Solo usas "The" si **especificas** exactamente de qué o de quién estás hablando (es decir, deja de ser general).
*   *General:* Music is good for you. (La música en general).
*   *Específico:* **The music** in this restaurant is loud. (La música específica de este lugar).
*   *General:* History is boring.
*   *Específico:* **The history of Rome** is fascinating.

---
Evita sobre-usar "The" en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: The love is all you need.
data.correct: Love is all you need.

data.incorrect: I read a book about life of Lincoln.
data.correct: I read a book about the life of Lincoln.

data.incorrect: Music of that movie is great.
data.correct: The music of that movie is great.

exercises.instruction: Gente en general (People, no the people).

data.incorrect: The people are crazy nowadays.
data.correct: People are crazy nowadays.

data.incorrect: He is love of my life.
data.correct: He is the love of my life.

content.explanation:
### Adjetivos con Preposiciones Fijas

Muchos adjetivos en inglés se "casan" con una preposición específica. ¡No las traduzcas de forma literal o cometerás errores! 

Aquí tienes las 7 combinaciones más críticas:
*   **Good/Bad AT:** Bueno/Malo en algo. (*I am good at math*. Nunca: *in math*).
*   **Afraid OF:** Tener miedo a. (*I am afraid of spiders*).
*   **Capable OF:** Capaz de. (*He is capable of murder*).
*   **Interested IN:** Interesado en. (*She is interested in art*).
*   **Married TO:** Casado con. (*He is married to my sister*. Nunca: *with*).
*   **Similar TO:** Parecido a. (*This is similar to that*).
*   **Proud OF:** Orgulloso de. (*I am proud of you*).

*Recuerda:* Si después de la preposición pones un verbo, **SIEMPRE debe ir en -ING**.
*   *I am good at **playing** tennis.*

---
Acopla estas parejas gramaticales.

data.incorrect: He is bad in sports.
data.correct: He is bad at sports.

data.incorrect: John is married with Mary.
data.correct: John is married to Mary.

data.incorrect: We are very proud for you.
data.correct: We are very proud of you.

data.incorrect: Your shirt is similar with mine.
data.correct: Your shirt is similar to mine.

data.incorrect: Paris is famous by its tower.
data.correct: Paris is famous for its tower.

content.explanation:
### Verbos con Preposiciones Fijas

Cerramos el centenar de unidades con un tema de dominio nativo. Al igual que los adjetivos, los verbos también tienen parejas fijas que no puedes separar.

Las combinaciones imprescindibles:
*   **Depend ON / Rely ON:** Depender de / Confiar en. (*It depends on the weather*. NUNCA *depends of*).
*   **Succeed IN:** Tener éxito en. (*She succeeded in passing the test*).
*   **Apologize FOR:** Disculparse por. (*I apologize for my mistake*).
*   **Complain ABOUT:** Quejarse de. (*He complains about everything*).
*   **Belong TO:** Pertenecer a. (*This book belongs to me*).
*   **Wait FOR:** Esperar por alguien. (*I am waiting for you*).
*   **Insist ON:** Insistir en. (*He insisted on paying*).

*Regla de Oro repetida:* Si usas un verbo después de la preposición, **¡Escríbelo en -ING!**

---
¡Celebra la Unidad 100 dominando estas combinaciones!

data.incorrect: He succeeded to win the race.
data.correct: He succeeded in winning the race.

data.incorrect: Stop complaining from the food.
data.correct: Stop complaining about the food.

data.incorrect: I apologize to my bad behavior.
data.correct: I apologize for my bad behavior.

data.incorrect: I am thinking in you.
data.correct: I am thinking about you.

data.incorrect: It depends of the money.
data.correct: It depends on the money.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con KEEP

El verbo "Keep" (Mantener/Guardar) tiene combinaciones muy útiles relacionadas con la constancia y la distancia.

1.  **Keep on:** Continuar haciendo algo (no rendirse).
    *   *Example:* Even when it's hard, you have to **keep on** trying.
2.  **Keep up with:** Mantener el ritmo (con alguien o con las noticias/tendencias).
    *   *Example:* He runs too fast, I can't **keep up with** him.
3.  **Keep away (from):** Mantenerse alejado (por peligro).
    *   *Example:* **Keep away from** the edge!
4.  **Keep out:** Prohibido el paso / No entrar.
    *   *Example:* The sign on the door says "**Keep out**".
5.  **Keep back:** Mantener distancia (usado por la policía), o retener una emoción/lágrimas.
    *   *Example:* The police told the crowd to **keep back**.
    *   *Example:* She tried to **keep back** her tears.

---
Mantén el buen ritmo en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: The sign says keep away.
data.correct: The sign says keep out.

data.incorrect: Keep out from the fire.
data.correct: Keep away from the fire.

exercises.instruction: Estar al día con las noticias.

data.incorrect: It is hard to keep on with the news.
data.correct: It is hard to keep up with the news.

data.incorrect: He kept in working late.
data.correct: He kept on working late.

data.incorrect: Keep the dogs away of my room.
data.correct: Keep the dogs out of my room.

content.explanation:
### Phrasal Verbs con HOLD

El verbo "Hold" (Sostener/Agarrar) forma frases vitales para comunicarse por teléfono y expresar retrasos.

1.  **Hold on:** Esperar (especialmente en el teléfono) o agarrarse fuerte.
    *   *Example:* Can you **hold on** a minute? (¿Puedes esperar?).
    *   *Example:* **Hold on** tight!
2.  **Hold up:** Retrasar algo (usualmente en el tráfico), o robar un banco/tienda con un arma.
    *   *Example:* I was **held up** in traffic.
    *   *Example:* Two men **held up** the bank.
3.  **Hold back:** Contenerse (emociones, información) o impedir que alguien avance.
    *   *Example:* Don't **hold back**, tell me the truth.
    *   *Example:* Fear is **holding** you **back**.
4.  **Hold off:** Posponer algo a propósito para ver qué pasa primero.
    *   *Example:* Let's **hold off** making a decision until tomorrow.

---
No te detengas y resuelve estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: I was held on in traffic.
data.correct: I was held up in traffic.

data.incorrect: Fear is holding you off.
data.correct: Fear is holding you back.

data.incorrect: Let's hold off to decide.
data.correct: Let's hold off deciding.

data.incorrect: The flight was held back by snow.
data.correct: The flight was held up by snow.

data.incorrect: Hold out a minute, please.
data.correct: Hold on a minute, please.

content.explanation:
### Inversión en Condicionales (Súper Formal)

En la Unidad 76 vimos que invertir el Sujeto y el Verbo da un toque muy formal y dramático. ¡También puedes hacerlo con los condicionales para **eliminar el "If"**!

Esto es lenguaje de nivel C2, muy común en correos de negocios y literatura.

#### 1er Condicional (Should)
*   *Normal:* **If you need** help, call me.
*   *Invertido:* **Should you need** help, call me. (Eliminas 'if' y pones 'Should' al principio).

#### 2do Condicional (Were)
*   *Normal:* **If I were** you, I wouldn't do it.
*   *Invertido:* **Were I** you, I wouldn't do it. (Inviertes 'Were' y el sujeto).
*   *Si hay otro verbo:* **Were I to win** the lottery, I would buy a house.

#### 3er Condicional (Had)
*   *Normal:* **If I had known**, I would have gone.
*   *Invertido:* **Had I known**, I would have gone.

---
Demuestra tu dominio absoluto del inglés en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: Was I you, I would leave.
data.correct: Were I you, I would leave.

exercises.instruction: No se usa 'If' con la inversión.

data.incorrect: If had I seen him, I would say hello.
data.correct: Had I seen him, I would have said hello.

exercises.instruction: Were I to (Para verbos en 2do cond).

data.incorrect: Were I go to Paris, I would be happy.
data.correct: Were I to go to Paris, I would be happy.

data.incorrect: Hadn't I seen it, I wouldn't believe it.
data.correct: Had I not seen it, I wouldn't have believed it.

exercises.instruction: Uso del would en el resultado.

data.incorrect: Had I money, I will buy it.
data.correct: Had I money, I would buy it.

content.explanation:
### Frases de Participio (Ahorrando palabras)

Para escribir de forma avanzada y concisa, solemos eliminar palabras como "who is" o "which was" (Cláusulas relativas) y dejamos solo el verbo en **-ING (Activo)** o **-ED (Pasivo)**.

#### Participio Presente (-ING)
Se usa si el sujeto está haciendo la acción activamente en ese momento.
*   *Largo:* The man **who is standing** there is my boss.
*   *Avanzado:* The man **standing** there is my boss. (El hombre parado allí...).
*   *Largo:* People **who arrived** late couldn't enter.
*   *Avanzado:* People **arriving** late couldn't enter.

#### Participio Pasado (-ED / V3)
Se usa si el sujeto recibe la acción pasivamente.
*   *Largo:* The book **which was written** by him is famous.
*   *Avanzado:* The book **written** by him is famous. (El libro escrito por él...).
*   *Largo:* Cars **that are parked** here will be towed.
*   *Avanzado:* Cars **parked** here will be towed.

---
Recorta las oraciones en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: The window breaking by him is fixed.
data.correct: The window broken by him is fixed.

data.incorrect: Products making in China are cheap.
data.correct: Products made in China are cheap.

data.incorrect: Anyone wished to leave may do so.
data.correct: Anyone wishing to leave may do so.

data.incorrect: The woman worn a red dress is Mary.
data.correct: The woman wearing a red dress is Mary.

data.incorrect: Cars stealing in the city are sold.
data.correct: Cars stolen in the city are sold.

content.explanation:
### Énfasis: So vs Such

Ambas palabras significan "Tan" o "Tanto" para exagerar algo, pero se usan con estructuras totalmente diferentes.

#### SO + Adjetivo / Adverbio (Solos)
Se usa cuando el adjetivo está solo, sin un sustantivo detrás.
*   *Example:* She is **so beautiful**. (Ella es tan hermosa).
*   *Example:* He runs **so fast**.

#### SUCH + (A/An) + Adjetivo + SUSTANTIVO
Se usa obligatoriamente si al final de la descripción hay un sustantivo (una cosa o persona).
*   *Example:* She is **such a beautiful girl**. (Ella es una chica tan hermosa).
*   *Incorrecto:* She is *so a beautiful girl*.
*   *Plural:* They are **such nice people**. (Sin "a/an" porque es plural).

*(Truco: Si hay un sustantivo al final, necesitas "Such").*

---
Pon a prueba tu énfasis en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: It was a so good movie.
data.correct: It was such a good movie.

data.incorrect: The test was such difficult.
data.correct: The test was so difficult.

data.incorrect: They are such a nice people.
data.correct: They are such nice people.

data.incorrect: It is so a beautiful day.
data.correct: It is such a beautiful day.

data.incorrect: It was a so long trip.
data.correct: It was such a long trip.

content.explanation:
### Comparaciones: As vs Like

Ambas se traducen como "Como", pero en inglés su uso es completamente diferente.

#### LIKE (Para similitudes)
Se usa para comparar a una persona/cosa con otra que es *similar*, pero que no es exactamente eso.
*   *Example:* He cries **like a baby**. (Él no es un bebé de verdad, pero llora de forma similar).
*   *Example:* You look **like your mother**. (Te pareces a ella).

#### AS (Para roles y profesiones)
Se usa para hablar de la función REAL de una persona o cosa.
*   *Example:* I work **as a teacher**. (Yo *soy* un profesor).
*   *Incorrecto:* I work *like* a teacher.
*   *Example:* I use this box **as a table**. (Esa es su función real ahora).

*(Recuerda: "Like" es para imitar, "As" es para ser).*

---
Diferencia los roles de las imitaciones en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: He cries as a baby.
data.correct: He cries like a baby.

data.incorrect: He was hired like a manager.
data.correct: He was hired as a manager.

data.incorrect: You look as your brother.
data.correct: You look like your brother.

data.incorrect: I want to fly as a bird.
data.correct: I want to fly like a bird.

data.incorrect: This meat tastes as chicken.
data.correct: This meat tastes like chicken.

content.explanation:
### Conectores de Razón y Resultado

Ya no eres un principiante, es hora de dejar de usar "Because" y "So" todo el tiempo.

#### Razones (Para sustituir Because)
*   **Since / As:** Se traducen como "Dado que" o "Ya que".
    *   *Example:* **Since** it's raining, we will stay home. (Dado que llueve...).
    *   *Example:* **As** you were out, I left a message.

#### Resultados (Para sustituir So)
*   **Therefore / Consequently:** (Por lo tanto / En consecuencia). Son muy formales y usan coma.
    *   *Example:* He didn't study. **Therefore,** he failed.
*   **So that:** (De manera que / Para que). Muestra el propósito de una acción, seguido de un verbo modal.
    *   *Example:* I spoke slowly **so that** they could understand me.

---
Argumenta como un abogado en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: I left early for that I arrive on time.
data.correct: I left early so that I arrive on time.

data.incorrect: Like you know, I am busy.
data.correct: As you know, I am busy.

data.incorrect: We canceled due to it rained.
data.correct: We canceled due to the rain.

data.incorrect: He is tired therefore he is sleeping.
data.correct: He is tired. Therefore, he is sleeping.

data.incorrect: Speak loud so as they hear you.
data.correct: Speak loud so that they hear you.

content.explanation:
### Comparativos Dobles (Mientras más... Más...)

En español decimos: "Mientras más estudias, más aprendes". 
En inglés, la palabra "mientras" desaparece y la estructura es: **The + Comparativo, The + Comparativo**.

*   *Example:* **The older** you get, **the wiser** you become. (Mientras más viejo te haces, más sabio te vuelves).
*   *Example:* **The more** you study, **the more** you learn.
*   *Example:* **The harder** he works, **the richer** he gets.

*(Nota: Tienes que usar el comparativo "-er" o "more" dependiendo de la longitud del adjetivo, como vimos en unidades básicas).*

#### La frase más famosa
*   **The sooner, the better:** Mientras más pronto, mejor.

---
Multiplica tus conocimientos en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: More you practice, more you learn.
data.correct: The more you practice, the more you learn.

data.incorrect: While more you eat, the fatter you get.
data.correct: The more you eat, the fatter you get.

exercises.instruction: Más duro trabajas, más cansado estás.

data.incorrect: The expensiver it is, the better it is.
data.correct: The more expensive it is, the better it is.

data.incorrect: The hot the weather, the better.
data.correct: The hotter the weather, the better.

data.incorrect: The more you give, more you receive.
data.correct: The more you give, the more you receive.

content.explanation:
### No importa qué (No matter vs Ever)

Para expresar "Sin importar lo que pase" o "Cualquier cosa", hay dos estructuras que significan exactamente lo mismo.

#### Forma 1: No matter + Palabra de pregunta
*   **No matter what:** No importa qué / Sin importar qué.
*   **No matter where:** Sin importar dónde.
*   **No matter who:** Sin importar quién.
    *   *Example:* **No matter what** you say, I won't go.

#### Forma 2: Palabra con -ever
Significan lo mismo, pero son una sola palabra y suenan un poco más elegantes.
*   **Whatever:** Lo que sea.
*   **Wherever:** Donde sea.
*   **Whoever:** Quien sea.
    *   *Example:* **Whatever** you say, I won't go.
    *   *Example:* You can sit **wherever** you want.

---
No importa qué elijas, hazlo bien en estos ejercicios.

data.incorrect: You can do no matter what you want.
data.correct: You can do whatever you want.

data.incorrect: I will follow you no matter to where you go.
data.correct: I will follow you wherever you go.

data.incorrect: Do it no matter how you like.
data.correct: Do it however you like.

exercises.instruction: Lo que sea necesario (Whatever it takes).

data.incorrect: I will do no matter what it takes.
data.correct: I will do whatever it takes.

data.incorrect: Whatever hard I try, I fail.
data.correct: No matter how hard I try, I fail.

content.explanation:
### Ya es hora: It's time...

Cuando sientes impaciencia y quieres decir "Ya es hora de que hagamos algo" (como una queja o algo que debió pasar hace rato), usas **It's time** o **It's high time**.

¡Aquí viene la trampa! Aunque estés hablando del presente, **el verbo que sigue debe ir en PASADO SIMPLE**. Es un uso subjuntivo/irreal (estás deseando que la situación ya hubiera ocurrido).

*   *Example:* **It's time** we **left**. (Ya es hora de que nos vayamos. NOTA: *left*, no *leave*).
*   *Example:* **It's high time** you **got** a job. (Ya va siendo hora de que consigas un trabajo).
*   *Example:* **It's about time** she **apologized**.

*(Nota: Si usas 'It's time TO', sí va el verbo normal: It's time to go).*

---
